description:
A FinOps analyst has noticed that several raw cloud-usage JSON files are wasting space.  
    All raw files live in  

        /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/

    What you must do
    ----------------
    1. Locate every “*.json” file in /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/ whose size is strictly **greater than 1 MiB (1 048 576 bytes)**.  
    2. Compress each of those files in-place with gzip so that every oversized file ends up as “<name>.json.gz” in the same directory (the original “*.json” file must disappear).  
    3. While doing the batch compression, append one CSV line per compressed file to the file  

           /home/user/cloud_costs/compression_report.csv  

       Each line must follow the exact format:  

           absolute_path_to_original_json,original_size_bytes,compressed_size_bytes  

       • use absolute path (not “./…”).  
       • original_size_bytes must be the size the file had **before** compression.  
       • compressed_size_bytes must be the size of the newly-created “.gz” file **after** compression.  
       • The file must end with a single newline and contain **only** the lines for the files you just compressed, sorted alphabetically by the JSON file’s absolute path.

    4. Complete everything with **one single compound command** that combines “find” and “xargs”. (Pipes, subshells, and “;” within that one liner are fine, but do not run multiple independent commands interactively.)

    What the grader will look for
    -----------------------------
    • Each *.json file larger than 1 MiB has vanished and an equivalently-named *.json.gz file is present in the same directory.  
    • /home/user/cloud_costs/compression_report.csv exists, is readable, has 600 permissions, and contains exactly one correctly-formatted, comma-separated line per compressed file — nothing more, nothing less.  
    • The “original_size_bytes” figure in every line exactly matches the size the file had before compression (the grader captured these values beforehand).  
    • The “compressed_size_bytes” figure exactly matches the current byte size of the corresponding “.gz” file and is strictly smaller than the original size.

    Remember: accomplish everything using a **single** command that relies on “find … | xargs …” (or “find … -print0 | xargs -0 …”) without manually iterating through the files.
truth:
Initial filesystem state (created for the agent before the task starts)
    ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    Directories (all already exist, 700 perms):
        /home/user/cloud_costs/
        /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/

    Oversized JSON files (need compression):
        1. /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/2023-11-01-usage.json
              size_before = 2 097 152  bytes  (2 MiB exact)
        2. /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/2024-01-01-usage.json
              size_before = 3 145 728  bytes  (3 MiB exact)
        3. /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/2024-01-15-usage.json
              size_before = 1 245 184  bytes  (≈1.188 MiB)

    Small JSON file (should NOT be touched):
        /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/2023-12-01-usage.json
              size = 512 000 bytes

    File that must be created by the agent:
        /home/user/cloud_costs/compression_report.csv
              permissions after task: 600 (rw-only for user)

    Expected end state after agent success
    --------------------------------------
    • The three oversized “*.json” files listed above no longer exist.
    • Corresponding files with “.json.gz” extension exist in the same directory.  
      The grader will record their actual sizes (all < size_before values).
    • /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/2023-12-01-usage.json still exists and is uncompressed.
    • /home/user/cloud_costs/compression_report.csv exists, 600 perms, contents:

        /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/2023-11-01-usage.json,2097152,<gz_size1>
        /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/2024-01-01-usage.json,3145728,<gz_size2>
        /home/user/cloud_costs/raw_usage/2024-01-15-usage.json,1245184,<gz_size3>

      where <gz_sizeN> is the exact byte size of each newly-created “.gz” file and each is strictly smaller than the corresponding “original_size_bytes”.

    The lines in the CSV are sorted alphabetically by the absolute JSON path (as shown above) and terminated by a single trailing newline.
name: task_000000_17d86e1f
